chapter_title: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes elements in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.
An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.

questions.explanation: An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with elements?

options.text: An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.
A sample of iron contains iron atoms.
Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.

questions.explanation: Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes elements from a nearby idea?

options.text: A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.
An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.

questions.explanation: An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of elements?

options.text: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.

questions.explanation: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about elements?

options.text: An atom is not necessarily the same as a molecule.
An element is not defined by whether it is solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature.
Every molecule is not a compound; some molecules contain one element only.
A compound cannot be separated into its elements by simple physical methods.

questions.explanation: An element is not defined by whether it is solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining elements. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.
An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.

questions.explanation: An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about elements?

options.text: Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.
A sample of iron contains iron atoms.
An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.
Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.

questions.explanation: Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of elements?

options.text: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.

questions.explanation: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A class records an observation related to elements. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.
Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.

questions.explanation: An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate elements?

options.text: Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.

questions.explanation: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes atoms in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.

questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with atoms?

options.text: A sample of iron contains iron atoms.
An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.
Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.
Air is a mixture of several gases.

questions.explanation: A sample of iron contains iron atoms.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes atoms from a nearby idea?

options.text: Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.
Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.

questions.explanation: Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of atoms?

options.text: Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.

questions.explanation: Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about atoms?

options.text: A mixture does not necessarily have a fixed composition.
Every molecule is not a compound; some molecules contain one element only.
A compound cannot be separated into its elements by simple physical methods.
An atom is not necessarily the same as a molecule.

questions.explanation: An atom is not necessarily the same as a molecule.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining atoms. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.
A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.

questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.

options.text: An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.

Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.

questions.explanation: Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.

questions.question_text: A class records an observation related to atoms. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes molecules in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.

questions.explanation: A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with molecules?

options.text: Air is a mixture of several gases.
Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.
An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.
Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.

questions.explanation: An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes molecules from a nearby idea?

options.text: A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.

questions.explanation: Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of molecules?

options.text: Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.

questions.explanation: Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about molecules?

options.text: A compound cannot be separated into its elements by simple physical methods.
Every molecule is not a compound; some molecules contain one element only.
A mixture does not necessarily have a fixed composition.
A property observed once under unknown conditions may not identify a substance conclusively.

questions.explanation: Every molecule is not a compound; some molecules contain one element only.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining molecules. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.

options.text: Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.

options.text: A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.

options.text: Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes compounds in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.

questions.explanation: A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with compounds?

options.text: Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.
Air is a mixture of several gases.
Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.

questions.explanation: Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes compounds from a nearby idea?

options.text: The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
The method must match the property difference between the components.

questions.explanation: A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of compounds?

options.text: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.

questions.explanation: Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about compounds?

options.text: No single separation method works for every mixture.
A mixture does not necessarily have a fixed composition.
A property observed once under unknown conditions may not identify a substance conclusively.
A compound cannot be separated into its elements by simple physical methods.

questions.explanation: A compound cannot be separated into its elements by simple physical methods.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining compounds. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.

questions.explanation: A compound is a pure substance formed when elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio.

Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.

options.text: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.

options.text: The method must match the property difference between the components.
The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
A compound has properties different from the elements that formed it.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate compounds?

options.text: Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.

questions.explanation: Distinguish a compound from a mixture by checking whether the substances are chemically combined.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes mixtures in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.

questions.explanation: A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with mixtures?

options.text: Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
Air is a mixture of several gases.
Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.

questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of several gases.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes mixtures from a nearby idea?

options.text: Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
The method must match the property difference between the components.
The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.

questions.explanation: The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of mixtures?

options.text: Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.

questions.explanation: Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about mixtures?

options.text: A property observed once under unknown conditions may not identify a substance conclusively.
A mixture does not necessarily have a fixed composition.
No single separation method works for every mixture.
A homogeneous mixture can still contain more than one substance.

questions.explanation: A mixture does not necessarily have a fixed composition.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining mixtures. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.

questions.explanation: A mixture contains two or more substances that are physically combined and can often be separated by physical methods.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about mixtures?

options.text: Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
Air is a mixture of several gases.

questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of several gases.

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of mixtures?

options.text: Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.

questions.explanation: Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.

options.text: A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
The substances in a mixture retain their identities and may occur in variable proportions.
The method must match the property difference between the components.
Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate mixtures?

options.text: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Choose a physical separation method based on a component property.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.

questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes physical properties in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.

questions.explanation: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with physical properties?

options.text: Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.

questions.explanation: Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes physical properties from a nearby idea?

options.text: The method must match the property difference between the components.
A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.

questions.explanation: A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of physical properties?

options.text: Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.

questions.explanation: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about physical properties?

options.text: A mixture need not be heterogeneous just because it contains several substances.
No single separation method works for every mixture.
A homogeneous mixture can still contain more than one substance.
A property observed once under unknown conditions may not identify a substance conclusively.

questions.explanation: A property observed once under unknown conditions may not identify a substance conclusively.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining physical properties. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.

questions.explanation: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance into a new substance.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about physical properties?

options.text: Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.

questions.explanation: Colour, state, solubility, and density are physical properties.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of physical properties?

options.text: Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.

questions.explanation: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A class records an observation related to physical properties. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.
The method must match the property difference between the components.
Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.

questions.explanation: A physical property describes a substance without requiring a chemical reaction.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate physical properties?

options.text: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.

questions.explanation: Compare samples using an agreed property such as solubility or magnetism.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes separation of mixtures in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.

questions.explanation: Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with separation of mixtures?

options.text: Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.
Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.

questions.explanation: Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes separation of mixtures from a nearby idea?

options.text: Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.
Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.
The method must match the property difference between the components.

questions.explanation: The method must match the property difference between the components.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of separation of mixtures?

options.text: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.

questions.explanation: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about separation of mixtures?

options.text: A homogeneous mixture can still contain more than one substance.
No single separation method works for every mixture.
A mixture need not be heterogeneous just because it contains several substances.
A component that is no longer visible has not necessarily been destroyed.

questions.explanation: No single separation method works for every mixture.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining separation of mixtures. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.

questions.explanation: Mixture components can be separated by using differences in physical properties.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about separation of mixtures?

options.text: Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.
Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.
Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.

questions.explanation: Filtration can separate an insoluble solid from a liquid.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of separation of mixtures?

options.text: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.

questions.explanation: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A class records an observation related to separation of mixtures. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
The method must match the property difference between the components.
Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.
Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.

questions.explanation: The method must match the property difference between the components.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate separation of mixtures?

options.text: Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.

questions.explanation: Select filtration, evaporation, sieving, or magnet separation for a particular mixture.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes homogeneous mixtures in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.
A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.

questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with homogeneous mixtures?

options.text: Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.
Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.
Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.

questions.explanation: Salt solution appears uniform after the salt dissolves.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes homogeneous mixtures from a nearby idea?

options.text: Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.
Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.
An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.

questions.explanation: Uniform appearance does not make a mixture a compound.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of homogeneous mixtures?

options.text: Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.

questions.explanation: Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about homogeneous mixtures?

options.text: An element is not defined by whether it is solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature.
A mixture need not be heterogeneous just because it contains several substances.
A component that is no longer visible has not necessarily been destroyed.
A homogeneous mixture can still contain more than one substance.

questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture can still contain more than one substance.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining homogeneous mixtures. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.
A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.
An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.

questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the sample at the scale being considered.

options.text: Take samples from different parts and compare their composition or properties.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes heterogeneous mixtures in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.
A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.

questions.explanation: A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition with distinguishable parts or regions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with heterogeneous mixtures?

options.text: Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.
A sample of iron contains iron atoms.

questions.explanation: Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes heterogeneous mixtures from a nearby idea?

options.text: Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.
An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.
Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.

questions.explanation: Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of heterogeneous mixtures?

options.text: Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.
Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.

questions.explanation: Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about heterogeneous mixtures?

options.text: A component that is no longer visible has not necessarily been destroyed.
A mixture need not be heterogeneous just because it contains several substances.
An element is not defined by whether it is solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature.
An atom is not necessarily the same as a molecule.

questions.explanation: A mixture need not be heterogeneous just because it contains several substances.

options.text: A sample of iron contains iron atoms.

Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
Sand in water forms a heterogeneous mixture.

Different parts can have visibly or measurably different compositions.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate heterogeneous mixtures?

options.text: Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.
Allow settling or use filtration when components have different observable phases.
Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes purity and conservation in nature of matter: elements, compounds, and mixtures?

options.text: A molecule is a group of atoms held together that behaves as a unit.
An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom.
An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical identity.
During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.

questions.explanation: During physical separation, the components of a mixture are recovered without being changed into new substances.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with purity and conservation?

options.text: Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.
Copper is an element used in electrical wiring.
A sample of iron contains iron atoms.
An oxygen molecule contains two oxygen atoms joined together.

questions.explanation: Evaporating salt water can recover salt while water leaves as vapour.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes purity and conservation from a nearby idea?

options.text: An element cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical methods.
Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.
Atoms are units of elements; they are not the same as a visible grain or piece of a substance.
Molecules may contain atoms of one element or of different elements.

questions.explanation: Recovering a component is different from destroying or creating matter.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of purity and conservation?

options.text: Use particle diagrams to represent an element as one type of atom.
Use a symbol and property list to identify an element in a material sample.
Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.
Read a simple molecular model by counting the kinds and numbers of atoms shown.

questions.explanation: Record the starting mixture and recovered fractions when evaluating a separation.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about purity and conservation?

options.text: Every molecule is not a compound; some molecules contain one element only.
An element is not defined by whether it is solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature.
An atom is not necessarily the same as a molecule.
A component that is no longer visible has not necessarily been destroyed.

questions.explanation: A component that is no longer visible has not necessarily been destroyed.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 8; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest
